name: House of Himwitsa
highlights: Nuu-chah-nulth-owned gallery showcasing West Coast Indigenous art since 1991
Paintings, carvings, jewelry, and weavings by artists from the five Tofino-area First Nations
Purchasing here directly supports Indigenous artists and community economies
One of the few places to buy authentic, artist-sourced Northwest Coast art in Tofino

name: Roy Henry Vickers Gallery
highlights: Tsimshian-Stolo artist Roy Henry Vickers's primary gallery since 1986
The gallery building itself is designed in traditional Northwest Coast form — a longhouse aesthetic
Limited-edition prints, original paintings, and ceremonial works
Vickers is among the most celebrated Indigenous artists in Canada

name: Tla-o-qui-aht Tribal Parks
highlights: In 1984, the Tla-o-qui-aht Nation declared Meares Island a Tribal Park — the first Indigenous land protection action in Canada
The declaration came in response to a logging company's plans to clear-cut the old-growth
76,000 hectares now protected under Indigenous governance across Clayoquot Sound
Traveling in this territory is entering actively stewarded Indigenous land — the ʔiisaak pledge invites respectful engagement
hours: Open territory — no visitor center; engage through tourismtofino.com
admission: free (water taxi to Meares Island has a fee)

name: Uʼmista Cultural Centre
location: Alert Bay (North Vancouver Island — accessible by ferry from Port McNeill)
highlights: Repository of Kwakwaka'wakw cultural heritage including the repatriated potlatch collection
The masks, coppers, and ceremonial objects here were confiscated by the Canadian government when potlatching was criminalized (1885–1951)
Uʼmista means 'the return of something important' — the centre is named for the return of these objects
One of the most significant Indigenous cultural institutions in Canada

name: Clayoquot Biosphere Trust
highlights: The science and stewardship body for the Clayoquot Sound UNESCO Biosphere Reserve
Supports research, education, and conservation across the 350,000-hectare reserve
Donations fund work that protects the old-growth, marine ecosystems, and Indigenous cultural landscapes you came to experience
Educational resources and local research available through the website
hours: Office hours vary — engage through website

name: The Butchart Gardens
location: Brentwood Bay — 30 min north of Victoria
highlights: 55 acres of five distinct gardens in a former limestone quarry — reclaimed since 1904
Jennie Butchart began transforming her husband's exhausted cement plant; the family still owns it
Over a million bedding plants in 900 varieties in continuous bloom from spring through fall
Saturday summer evenings include fireworks and live music; afternoon tea in the historic dining room
hours: Year-round; hours vary by season — check butchartgardens.com
